I	N T	H	E
T	R	E	N	C	H	E	S
R	E	AL
B	U	S	I	NE	S	S
OW NE	R	S
J	U	G	G	L	I	NG
A L	OT
OFB	AL	L	S
F	r	o	m T	h	eG	K	I	C	/
D	a	nK	e	n	n	e	d	yA	r	c	h	i	v	e	s
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 14 --

1 | P a g e
DECEMBER 2013
“JUGGLING A LOT OF BALLS” TIME MANAGEMENT
With Dan Kennedy, Ben Glass and Steve Adams
Dan Kennedy: Good afternoon everybody, Dan Kennedy here. I have two great guests with me who we’ll get to in just a
moment. As I pointed out to them and to the operator, I saw no point in starting a call somewhat having to do with time
management perfectly on time! So we began a few minutes late and we may very well, because of my schedule, end a
couple of minutes early but there we go.
I do want to tell you that we have now a number of really good and a couple of mediocre entries for Diamond Member of
the Year. I will be communicating with finalists immediately after the Thanksgiving Weekend so you will have ample time
to prepare for the December call where the final bloodletting will occur. Someone will qualify for an expenses-paid trip to
Winners Weekend in February in Orlando, this year at the Disney Yacht Club where you will be joined by Titanium
Member of the Year, Info-marketer of the Year, Marketer of the Year etc. etc. plus several top GKIC affiliates as well for
a mastermind day and a couple of great dinners and a little time at Epcot I believe is the way the agenda is shaping up. If
you have not entered and are still interested in doing so, you have until the end of the day tomorrow. [Entries are now
closed.]
The other thing I have to do is remind you guys there is a December 22 nd discount-ending, price-increasing occurrence
related to my Advanced Wealth Attraction Academy and my Craftsmanship of One-to-Many Selling Presentations training
occurring in June of next year. If you’ve been tardy about that, on December 22 nd – assuming there are still positions left
and right now they are nearly 70% sold out – the fee will increase.
Now to the last of our little series of time management and productivity, getting your act together, functioning as a
Schizophrenic and multiple ball-juggling entrepreneur calls. I have with me today two of my great Titanium members, Ben
Glass and Steve Adams. In the unlikely event that there are people on the call who are not familiar with them, I’m going
to let them each do their own little thumbnail introduction of who they are, where they came from and what they do.
Then, as these are two of the busiest guys I know, basically running multiple businesses – one in the professional service
category and one in the retail category – as well of course as info-marketing, we will have a conversation about how
they keep all of those balls in the air and maintain their sanity, and, as Ben says in his book title, still manage to get
home at night for dinner on time. So Ben, why don’t you go first?
Ben: Thanks Dan. I’m a full-time practicing personal injury and medical malpractice attorney; I’ve been doing that about
30 years. I employ a bunch of people including two other lawyers; it’s a very busy practice as you can imagine. As we
were discussing in Cleveland recently Dan, it was about 15 or 16 years ago that I discovered you, switched all of our
marketing to Dan Kennedy style marketing and really not only grew the practice but then, out of that, grew an information
marketing business that we call Great Legal Marketing. That will have its eighth birthday coming up in February. We
really have lawyers across the United States and Canada – and now some members internationally – who listen to us; it’s
pretty much GKIC-style information marketing business. My little joke is that, as an entrepreneur, I get to work whatever
80 hours a week I feel like working! I employ a bunch of people in that business.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 14 --

2 | P a g e
Most importantly, I am the father to nine – three are up and out in the world, one is in college and five are still at home.
Four of those are adopted from China; two have pretty significant what we call psychological needs related to being born
and living their whole lives in an orphanage. That requires a lot of very focused parenting; it requires relearning parenting
and a lot of time, energy, resources devoted to counsellors and stuff like that. So I feel it’s a very blessed, rich life; I have
a lot of opportunities but I do have a lot of strategies for, as you say, getting it all done.
Dan: I’ve never asked you, do you have a hobby?
Ben: I probably read about four or five books a month; I’ve run marathons; I referee soccer games. I don’t play golf; I
don’t watch much TV at all. My hobby actually is to always be learning this stuff because this is the stuff, the marketing,
the thrill of the kill is what gets me up at 4.30 in the morning and turns me on. That’s kind of weird but the people I’ve
met in GKIC and in the Titanium group, that’s kind of how they are!
Dan: Alright; Steve?
Steve: Thank you Dan. I’ve only been in the GKIC world now for about 2½ years. Somehow I managed to not find Dan
for the first 16 years of my business but I’ve been in the Pet Supplies Plus business – we have 21 stores in four states and
about 400 employees. Prior to that I was a corporate lender and I worked my way up to president of a bank in my mid-
30s. I did that side-by-side with the retail business for a while until I finally realized that I was way too much of an
entrepreneur to work in the regulated world of commercial banking.
I am also working on an information business on top of our retail business. I’ve really enjoyed learning the direct response
GKIC-style marketing that Dan teaches. In our retail setting, we’ve been able to apply that pretty effectively. We’ve
enjoyed double-digit same-store sales growth rates for each of the last three years in an industry where 5% is considered
really good; so I’m excited about that. I published a book this year called The Passionate Entrepreneur and, in 2014, I’m
going to be trying to launch an information business off that as well. So I’m happy to be here.
Dan: I just want to ask you a curiosity question – a broad general question – how crazy is it to try to run 21 stores spread
out over four states?
Steve: It would be crazy if I tried to do it all myself and I’d be lying if I didn’t say that there was a time when I was,
before we had enough scale. But it can be challenging; I don’t know what the number is but it is something in the order of
4,000 customer visits a day so there is a lot of opportunity to screw up and have problems. We’ll get into it more later but
it’s really about distributed leadership and empowering people and holding them accountable.
Dan: Okay cool. I’m just going to throw broad ones and let you guys kind of go where you want. But I can assure you
what people want to know is how you are able to manage these multiple functions and far-flung enterprises and people
effectively without the quality of what you do going to hell in a hand-basket – and I know both of you to be quality-
concerned people. Steve you are doing more in your stores than probably anybody else is with pet stores with nutritional
counselling and concierge service and all of that. Ben, your information business is one of the best run, delivers the best
quality, has the highest satisfaction levels and all of that. So when you keep piling on as we entrepreneurs do and here’s
all we’re doing and now we’re going to do this thing and then we’re going to do this next thing and you add people, what
are the top one or two or three things you’ve learned over time that you didn’t know early about keeping a grip on it all?
Ben: The biggest thing that I didn’t know is the whole mindset thing; it is realizing – particularly in the law practice –
that it’s okay to not be everything for everybody and so being very selective in whom we are going to work with in the
law practice and what the terms of engagement are. When you grow up in the law, it’s like you are to be of service to
everyone. So it was giving myself permission to recognize that this is my life. I have two main responsibilities one is to
secure the financial security of my family; the other is to secure the emotional security of my family.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 14 --

3 | P a g e
If, heaven forbid, something happens to me tonight, I don’t want my family to be the one that says, “Oh we never saw
him.” So those are my two top focuses and then, realizing that, if you set the terms of engagement, people will honor that.
Dan, everything I know about this I’ve learned from you but our mantra here in both of the businesses is “This is how it
works here.” This is how you interact with Ben, this is how you get an appointment or get a phone call with him; nothing
is ad hoc, nothing is at random.
This is how it is. You can engineer your businesses that way if everything is fuelled by great marketing of course. So
there’s always a line in both businesses of people waiting to get in and they have to do it under our terms. That was big
and the little subset of that is the whole managing the phone thing.
Dan: Can you recall when you first started to make this transition from fear of loss of a client by not being immediately
accessible, peer pressure about how a practice is supposed to function, to these decisions?
Ben: Well I said I found you 15 years ago and, not long into reading your philosophy, I said, “That makes a lot of sense,
let’s do it”. So, for example, we have no unplanned inbound phone calls. When I said that to my staff, they said that’s
crazy, that will never work, what if so-and-so calls or a claims adjuster or a judge calls? I said well if they called and I
wasn’t here, they wouldn’t be able to get me. So I’m not here and during certain times they are just off limits. I do think
that has to be fuelled by a confidence that you’ve got a line of people waiting to see you. But, even before I was all the
way there on the marketing side, I just started to take that position and Robert Ringer’s book and your philosophies
emboldened me to do that.
Dan: Cool. Steve, do you want to take a whack at it?
Steve: I have a little bit different situation than Ben in that my clients don’t really have easy access to me. I don’t have
that problem because we have hundreds of thousands of them but what I do have – that some other people may be able to
relate – is more if you have multiple locations or you have distance.
I had to attack it from two levels and I knew none of this 15 years ago. On a personal level, I had to get myself very
organized otherwise the rest of my organization would be chaotic. So first of all, I have a very extreme sense of time’s
value and I have a lot of urgency around time. That one movie – I can’t remember the name of it – where everybody has a
certain amount of time to live and they had to fight for more time comes to mind.
I am really clear about the outcomes I want and then, every year about this time of year, I write a personal business plan
for myself. I’ll go in to it later how I drive that down my organization. Then I plan my whole year out and from there I try
to write a whole week to two weeks' worth of day's time-blocked looking at my broader goals and making sure they are
showing up in the stuff that I’m doing each week.
When I did all of that, that’s when I started to get personally very effective. From an organizational standpoint, I had to
also develop a lot of really strong leaders in our company who could also think like this so that I could distribute the
thinking and the decision-making. Then we put a pretty robust process behind that where I know the execution is
happening. So every leader in our company, every store manager, every regional manager has a business plan for their
unit. They have quarterly goals and I have dashboards for all the stores and the regions and I’m able to look at their
leading indicators.
That’s how I ensure the quality, going back to your question Dan, stays there. We’ve learned to measure what are the key
indicators of the quality we want and then we have a process to keep that going. So that would be my answer to that. For
me, it really is a challenge because one of the things I didn’t say in my intro is I’m leading a non-profit where we do
micro-enterprise lending. We are in two countries now and we’re going to open five more next year so I’m doing that in
addition to this and everything is far away. So I have to think like this all the time.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 14 --

4 | P a g e
Dan: Tell me just a little bit – because it’s my experience a lot of people don’t understand it at all – and an example or
two if you can, the difference between a leading indicator and a trailing indicator.
Steve: Sure, I’ll think about the trailing ones. Are you relating this back to the issue of how you maintain quality when
being crazy busy?
Dan: Quality or revenue or profit; whatever.
Steve: Well for us, in our business, the key numbers are same-store sales growth, customer-churn percentage and client
satisfaction scores – those are the top three things I look at. If a store has got a low churn rate and high scores on
satisfaction and good year-on-year sales growth, that tells me that the overall culture is healthy in the store.
We do a couple of other things; we actually grade our employees A, B and C and do a grade-point average like your
grades in school. That gets updated every month and if a store’s GPA falls below 3.0 we know that our manager there has
got turnover problems because a lot of new employees grade out low and then grade higher as we go. It’s interesting you
ask that question because we are actually enhancing our dashboard to find cultural leading indicators and put them on
there but those would be some examples.
Dan: So when you start to now plan for 2014. I’ll come back to Ben first and I’ll assume you are planning for 2014! What
does that look like for you? What’s your approach?
Ben: For us, the two businesses are different but they are the same. They really are about for us creating new ways for
people to notice us and start to pay attention to us and come in to a funnel.
I think what we’re really good at, what I’m really good at… in my two businesses, the total number of people under me is
probably 20 but Steve said he needs to have people think like he does. That’s been a real key for us; as we sit down and
think what does the end of 2014 look like? When we look back, what will we have accomplished? I can only do that if I’m
growing… I like to say I’m growing entrepreneurs in both businesses who can think like me. So we have such a high
focus on not only “What do we do?” but “Why do we do things this way?” What would Ben say and what’s the way that
we can deliver the greatest client experience?
One part of that whole process is, for example, in the law practice, all the calls are recorded; all the inbound calls are
recorded. So I have someone where one of her primary responsibilities is to go back every week and listen to those calls;
think about that – to listen to the inbound calls, to make sure that what I think is happening is actually happening. I
empower her, she is trained, she understands how I think and she takes care of this for me. So that’s what we do. We are
in a constant state of training people who don’t need to come to Ben to ask the answers to questions and we are in a
constant state of quality control.
Dan: So will you lay out goals and targets and benchmarks for each person, for the group, for little groups by segment?
How do you get a year’s targets and progress put into effect?
Ben: Each of my groups is relatively small so I don’t have subsets of groups. It’s more “Let’s identify the key things”. So
the metrics in a law practice are how many leads are we getting? How many convert to information requesters? How
many convert to clients? How long does it take them to wind their way through the process?
So there’s a lot of data within there that we can measure. We can say this needs to improve and here are the 18 steps to
improve let’s say the web experience. That’s a great place because there’s so much data available from people who come
to our website – there are all sorts of metrics there.
That’s the way we do it because I can meet with the whole group in either of the businesses at the same time. It’s not
really divided into subgroups. Because of the Titanium meeting, we are bringing on in the info-marketing businesses
more
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 14 --

5 | P a g e
folks who devote themselves totally to sales – that’s your job, sales. So we’ll need to develop the exact metrics there but
to date it’s been more group global than very specific.
Dan: Steve, same question for you. As you go to lay out 2014, what does that look like for you?
Steve: Well first of all, we define what the outcome is we want and our goal every year is double-digit growth. So we
define our top line and what our budget is going to look like for the year. As I said, I do my own personal business plan
but we have each one of the store directors write a one-page business plan and that’s all they get is one-page. We’ve
learned in the past that if it’s short and very concise they’ll execute on it. So it’s a one-page business plan built around our
playbook; we have six things and six things only that our store managers focus on and they have to build it within that.
They also update with their regional manager a scorecard that has the key drivers that they control in their store. The
regional manager and store manager meet every October, November and work through a business plan and update the
scorecard. The business plan is prioritized by quarter. Then, every month, one of their playbooks – one of the six items in
there is they have a monthly action plan for their staff and goal plan for the store. Then backing that up further, on a
quarterly basis, myself and the regional manager meet with each store manager separately and go through how they did –
how did they do in April, how did they do in the first quarter? It’s a coaching session towards the second quarter.
That’s how we do the year is the scorecards, the business plans, the budgets. By the end of that process, everybody is very
aligned around what our big goals as a company are for the following year.
Then what I do personally is, in December, I get everything I think I’m going to do and I put it on my calendar for the
whole year and see what it looks like, to see if I’ve overcommitted myself. If I have, I start pulling things out of it so that
I’ve got adequate time balance between travelling and being in my office and doing what I do best. So really beginning
with the end in mind – as Covey taught – and working back from there.
Dan: A micro question; Ben raised the issue… the part of management I always say people like the least is the police job;
the enforcement job. So are you mystery shopping your stores and your phones?
Steve: Yes; we don’t do phones and that’s probably a weakness we need to address but we have a company that secret
shops every single store in our company at different rates depending on how well that staff is performing. It’s a pretty
exhaustive list of things they are going in and evaluating. Then we also have the client satisfaction survey scores that we
are looking at.
That’s how we enforce it. We also enforce it just with a lot of visibility in the stores by our regional guys. Myself and one
of my partners, we travel to see stores ourselves but it also has a lot to do with how we hire. We have a very good
assessment tool that measures how people work naturally and if they do not fit within what we’ve deemed as the ideal
profile we will not hire them. So we end up only interviewing two out of 20 people that apply for jobs. We went through
that about four years ago and that really helped simplify the enforcement. When we hire people on profile, they don’t need
to be motivated; there is a certain DNA in them that they have to achieve and that makes the enforcement job a lot easier.
Dan: What percentage of your entire workforce has some incentive compensation – it either has primary pay or over and
above base?
Steve: It’s primarily our top two people in each store. We call it is our entrepreneurial pay program and it’s uncapped.
As they drive topline growth in their store and maintain their controllables; they get fairly large increases in their salary.
It’s basically a gain-sharing program and it’s at risk. So, if they let their store fall apart, they lose it.
We’ve had three or four managers increase their pay by 30 to 40,000 within the span of 12 to 15 months in the last three
years. I think this year will be the first year we have one that’s six figures. In our size of store typically it’s a $40,000 to
$45,000 a year job and we are going to have someone making six figures this year.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 14 --

6 | P a g e
What we found though with the hourly staff is that it is very difficult for them to relate their part-time job with the drivers
that we could pay them with. So there what we do is we just pay them on the generous side of the market and we create a
nice environment for them. We found that has worked best.
Dan: I want to go back to this for a second with you Ben and the recorded phone calls and the person whose job it is to
check them. Do you have any other kind of monitoring going on besides that and what do you do when you find the phone
is not being handled as you intended?
Ben: There is no other monitoring besides me occasionally hearing what’s going on but I’m only hearing one side of the
conversation – if we’re talking about phone calls specifically.
Dan: Anything but phone?
Ben: The law practice is small and I see everything that comes through here. I’m in many if not most of the client
meetings. There aren’t as many moving parts as you might have in a retail store like Steve has. But I think it’s important;
the second part of the question is very important. I learned a lot from Keith Lee in Titanium and his personal development
interview system. So what we do – this is kind of cool – is every week in the law practice, we have one or two team
meetings. Any of the correspondence that’s come in, emails that they need my advice on, that all gets clumped together
and now we are discussing them as a group at the meeting. Again, the main purpose is everyone understands what we do
and why we are doing it this way.
So it’s really a teaching opportunity and it’s the same thing so if I get a report saying here’s two calls that weren’t handled
right, I’ll say, “Suzie, we got two calls. Remember the guy that called about such and such? Here’s what he said and
here’s what you said. Here’s the way I think that we ought to do it. What does everybody think about this?” Of course, a
lot of the times, they’ll have better ideas than I do.
We are always trying to build people up. My folks are good because they’ve gone through a vetting process to even get
here – like Steve described. We use Jay Henderson – we rely on him a lot to help us vet people. But I view my role as to
teach because the better they are, the less time I’m in here actually working on legal stuff; I’m doing the stuff that is more
fun for me which is creating marketing, creating follow-up sequences, making that stuff as complicated and sophisticated
as I possibly can.
Dan: Going back to where we ended about how much time you are able to spend doing x versus y, I’ll stay with Ben and
then I’ll come to you Steve. People are going to be very curious about what your schedule looks like, how you are
allocating your time and how you are managing to be at home for the orphanage or dinner or whatever it is that you are
doing. So Ben, take us through a week.
Ben: Maybe I have no hobbies and I’m a boring guy because I’m usually asleep by 9.30 at night. But I’m a 4.30 to 4:45
guy in the morning. My first kids who are coming down to eat breakfast are coming down at 5.30 so typically I will have
preplanned what I’m going to do in that hour or 45 minutes so there’s no decision-making. It might be running in
appropriate weather but it’s usually writing something or reading something.
People say all the time how do you split your time? Well with these two businesses, there is such a blend. In a week, I’m
typically working directly on client stuff no more than 3 to 5 hours. I have really honed that down to where I’m applying
my expertise at the right place in a case and that’s the sale of the firm to a client; that’s thinking about the strategies that
others in my office are going to, in large part, execute.
We’ll have one or two of these team meetings in the law practice and that really is all hands on deck – it’s the marketing
people, the people doing the quality controls, the paralegals – they’re all there because we all want to be on the same page.
In fact I have a separate physical office in another building in my complex that’s totally devoted to marketing so mainly
the Great Legal Marketing stuff goes on there but also all of the marketing for Ben Glass Law. So we have a video studio.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 14 --

7 | P a g e
I’m meeting that team probably three times a week – we just had a meeting this morning with a graphic designer in my
team and we just went through about 10 projects in both businesses of what’s launching and it’s all marketing.
I don’t go out for lunch much; I’m typically eating at my desk and then typically, at the end of the day, there’s some kid
that needs to be picked up from a soccer field at 6 or 6.30 or something like that. It’s true that in my family most every
night we are eating dinner at home together and that is critically important for us. I really don’t do work after dinner; I try
to be present. I really don’t work on weekends except for I’ll still get up early. I get up at the same time Saturday and
Sunday and typically I’m writing or reading something that has to do with marketing because that’s the thing that turns
me on the most. That’s what it looks like.
The one other thing I would add which people may find helpful is the way Ben’s calendar is managed. It’s my
responsibility to get into the calendar and to grab all those slots that I want for myself. So if I’m going to be in Cleveland,
if I’m going to be on this call, if I’m going to be exercising or going to be writing a brief or whatever, those come off the
table. Now my staff has authority to schedule around my huge time blocks and that’s the only time so they don’t schedule
things for me at night or after dinner; they don’t schedule things for me on Tuesdays because Tuesday is my day where
it’s inviolate. They can’t have Tuesday for anything. So really we are talking about a four-day work week. My marketing
team will say we’ve scheduled you a block to come into the video studio and these are the videos we’re going to do and
here’s the script were going to do. Because they think like me already and I just show up and go do it. That’s what it looks
like and I’m exhausted at 9.30. I go to sleep; it’s a rich life.
Steve: Well, I was looking forward to that answer with nine kids; that’s a lot! I do some of the same things Ben does. For
me, I have organized my business so that I’ve got three teams that implement everything; I’m out of the implementation
business, if you will, now. I realize everybody on the call may not have that luxury but there was a day when I was.
I really hate meetings and so Monday is the day. Even though I love what I do, Mondays are Mondays so I put meetings
on Mondays. That’s the only time that people internally can meet with me unless it’s something very specific on a project.
So we get those out of the way that day then on Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday I am able to spend all of my time
focused on the areas that I can have a big impact. Those areas in our company are the marketing and I do a lot of
leadership development and training of our team. Then also communicating what our culture is so that the experience that
our clients have in the store is consistent all the time. That’s something I’ve learned you can never let up on.
Like Ben, I’m up early – I can’t do 4.30 but I’m up usually at 5.30 and I’m done working every day by six and home for
dinner. I don’t work the weekends; when I do work on weekends it’s mainly just catching up on reading. So my schedule
is pretty standard 7 to 5.30. I’m up early at home doing some reading.
I do a lot of travel because of my international work in addition to seeing the stores. It’s funny, a lot of people that I talk to
they just automatically project onto me that “You must just be crazy busy. You must never see your wife.” They have all
these illusions in their head and I say, “No, I’m home for dinner every day and I don’t work weekends”. I’m pretty
balanced; I only have two kids but they are both in college and have lots of needs still. My son’s a college baseball player
so there are lots of places I have to go and travel and watch him play. It’s nothing magical; it’s pretty boring actually. I
tend to theme my days and work on similar things each day, week after week after week so I get into a rhythm.
Dan: In just a few minutes, I’m going to go to Q&A but I have two more questions for you guys and both I think are
relatively short, quick and dirty. One is tell me about your handling of connectivity and technology – meaning email,
Twitter, Facebook, cellphone.
Steve: I don’t do Twitter; actually I do but it’s automated by a software tool. I don’t do Facebook at all; I look at it once
every three weeks and my daughter gets mad at me because I don’t respond sometimes. Email – it’s there, I use it but it’s
in my time blocking, I check it twice a day. I have to fight all the time sliding back to looking at it repeatedly during the
day but, for the most part, I only deal with it periodically. Then with the cellphone, it’s the same thing. I only answer the
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 14 --

8 | P a g e
phone and check those things once or twice a day and it never is something I react to. That would be true of any of that; I
don’t react to any of it – it’s all scheduled and in its place.
Ben: There are only about 11 people in the world that have my cell phone number so that’s not a problem and most of
them are my kids! We have Twitter accounts but that is all managed by my folks who are rewriting stuff I’ve done in the
past. I have a personal Facebook page that I attend to a little bit.
The important thing about email is slowing down what’s going to happen next. Our clients by and large do not email me
because that’s not how it’s done here. But even if they did it gets shuffled off during one of our team meetings to one of
my assistants and I’ll say, “Here is the response, you can get back to Dan here” but it’s not coming back from me.
In the Great Legal Marketing side, I don’t think anyone has ever emailed me because they know I am the last guy to know
anything like logistics; that could actually answer one of their questions. So I think that’s how you manage that. I don’t
watch TV much at all; I read a ton but electronics is pretty well controlled.
Dan: Okay last question. What haven’t I asked you that you maybe presumed I would ask or think I should have asked
that you wanted to talk about?
Ben: I think it’s critically important – I learned this from Lee Milteer when we were in Peak Performers together – and
what every high performer that I know does, is they have time integrity with themselves. So they are blocking out what
Lee called some “thinking days” so I’ll use that term as well where I go to a cave. I actually have several offices that have
no Internet, no email, no telephone but I lease them. Sometimes we meet clients there but they are my cave and, if I go
there, nobody can even reach me if they want to reach me. So it’s important I think. High performers will block out this
time in advance – you’re going right now to your 2014 calendar and saying these are the days when I will be thinking
about my business or whatever problem you need to solve. Totally it’s as though I’m on a ship in the Mediterranean; I
cease to exist from the world.
Steve: That was the first thing that came to my mind too was exactly that but the other thing that came to mind is that I
think all of us who are entrepreneurs are big thinkers and want to try to do big things. The thing that I wrote down that we
didn’t talk about or I wasn’t asked about was how do you manage several big visions at once? The thing I really work at is
that I am always looking for opportunities to leverage me. If I’ve got something new I’m working on, I’m always looking
for that person who needs a win, who is ready to step up and take the next level of responsibility and putting them in that
position and then working through that person rather than me doing it. At my age and where I’ve been, the best thing I
can always do is guide and direct and lead and let others implement. So that would be the other thing is looking for these
points of leverage around you.
Dan: By the way, Ben you were in Peak for a year, two years?
Ben: Yes early days, first couple of years.
Dan: Steve, were you in Peak?
Steve: Yes for one year and then I jumped into your group.
Dan: So, for Diamond members on the call who are not in any of our mastermind groups, you’ve just heard some very
smart, sophisticated people who have benefited from being in Peak Performers and I would suggest to you that that’s a
very good place to at least go and put in a year or two.
Ben: Dan, could I echo that? I just want to say that everyone in the GKIC world should spend a year with Lee Milteer.
She really does great work on your mindset.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 14 --

9 | P a g e
Dan: While we are setting up the questions, I did want to thank everyone who was at the Info-SUMMIT SM . By all accounts
and all feedback I’ve gotten and all observation, as well as our statistical metrics, it was just a great event.
QUESTIONS
Dwight: This is a great call for me because I’m actually in the process of hiring new people but my question is, when I’m
listening to Dr. Tom Orent’s presentation, I get this idea can I try to sell martial arts classes after doing a scheduled 30-
minute interview on the phone like the way he does with his Gems people? Do you think that is a crazy idea since they
will ultimately have to come in and join the school? But sometimes people hesitate to come to our initial consultation so
what if I send them a whole bunch of stuff – video and otherwise – and then schedule a call?
Dan: Can I just ask a clarifying question before they weigh in? Is your interest in doing this as a substitute for a face-to-
face meeting?
Dwight: No it’s an idea that hatched itself I guess just for the more stubborn prospects.
Dan: So it’s about somebody that is recalcitrant about coming in. Ben obviously you meet with clients one-on-one, Steve
your guys essentially sell one-to-one in-store. You both undoubtedly have some awareness of what a martial arts school is
like. So have at it.
Ben: I don’t know what the lifetime value of the client is but I would think that doing 30-minute one-on-one interviews
without some sort of prequalification, you would want to be shooting yourself after a month of that. So let me tell you
what we do and Dan helped a lot with this because kind of a variant in the legal practice and professional practice is “I
hired Ben Glass and I want Ben Glass to work on my case.” So we do what you are trying to do through DVDs, through
CDs, through a true GKIC kill-em shock-and-awe package.
Now Dr. Tom is selling a very high-end coaching program but I think, if you cornered him, he would tell you that it’s
exhausting. The other thing that is important to know is that there’s got to be a pre-qualification to that live event if you’re
going to do it. His is they have to fill out a pretty extensive survey so it kind of depends on if you tell me it’s a $60,000 a
year martial arts student but I think you can do a lot of this.
We created the “This is how it works here at Ben Glass Law” DVD set to sell the firm but also to manage the expectation
that, just because Ben is your lawyer does not mean that Ben knows every little thing that is going on in your case
yesterday. That’s not my job.
Steve: I can handle a little but our businesses are so different in an appropriate way here. The one thing we are doing that
might be similar to Dwight is we have these certified nutritionists in our stores and we are trying to get more people to
take us up on a 40-minute consultation. I think we are going about it as Ben described and that is creating a DVD that tells
them all about it before they do it.
We use two-step lead generation where they request the DVD and then we send it to them. What we found in our
generalized marketing is that it’s not enough of a hot button to get people to come in; but when they do come in and do it
they are really excited about it. But, again, we don’t have time to be on the phone with everybody talking to them – there
are too many people.
Dwight: Yes I’m still going to push for the face-to-face meeting but it was just a very intriguing thought; if they hesitate,
what if I offer them a scheduled time where I’ll get on the phone and answer all their questions but supplement that with
sending them something in the mail.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 14 --

10 | P a g e
Dan: I think that’s key; they’ve got to have a package and maybe go through some sort of diagnostic thing obviously – but
simpler than what Tom does. Last time I talked to Tom, that prospect is now spending 2½ hours filling out that
questionnaire. But I think they’ve got to be getting presentations and somehow jump through a hoop before they get time
on the phone.
------------------------
Jeff: I’ll just say it was a great Info-SUMMIT SM and thanks for all the great advice I’ve gotten from your group. I do have
a question on the hiring process. You mentioned that you use a Jay Henderson. You also mentioned that you have some
other kind of process. I hire a lot of people and I didn’t know if you had a name for that process that you use. I’d very
much want to have it so that I get the right people on the bus and make sure that I avoid some of the people that I’ve hired
in the past.
Dan: Ben why don’t you go first and talk about both Jay and how your Bar Association considers you evil because of
what you make the fledgling attorney go through? Then we’ll have Steve talk about his process.
Ben: Well it’s not anything really complicated but one of the things that we’ve done really well is, when we advertise for
a position, we are using in essence long form sales letters. If you’re familiar with them, Dan can give you the reference to
the letter Gary Halbert wrote to find his girlfriend many years ago. So it was forming in your head what that perfect
employee looks like for that position and really not advertising a job that says “Front Desk Clerk – send me your resume”.
It’s marketing.
Jay Henderson’s company is called Real Talent (www.RealTalentHiring.com) and he’s got a test that I and everyone on
my team has taken to learn how to work with each other and to learn how each of us thinks and produces. We found it
amazingly accurate so it’s one thing that we do. Other than that, it’s a couple of interviews, call the references and stuff
like that.
But for us, it begins with what we call our “Killer Craigslist Ads” which really read more like a personals column ad than
a typical – I don’t know what your business or profession is – but a typical position-seeking ad in your business. You just
have to be different.
Dan: Jay by the way is joining us in Titanium next year. Ben, do you have an ad up now?
Ben: Well actually we sold a bundle; we don’t have any ads up now but if you go to GLMWebstore.com, you can search
for Killer Craigslist Ads. I think it was $25 and it’s one of the best things we’ve ever done.
Steve: We have a tool called “Work Styles” and the developer of that product is a guy by the name of Denny Howard
from Fort Wayne, Indiana and I’m just going to give you his number 260-493-0055 (www.findyourlivstyle.com). What it
does is it measures the way people work naturally and what you want to do is you want to hire people who are a fit with
the type of work you do so that they are not fighting themselves on the inside.
We have a complete funnel that we’ve built out with the Infusionsoft folks where, whether it’s a Craigslist or a
Monster.com ad, the copy is very much written in a way that targets the kind of person we want and it repels the people
that we don’t want. That’s worked real well for us so the kind of people we want respond to the ad and then the funnel
kicks them into the tool; they take the assessment and if they pass, that gets kicked to the store manager who then
interviews them.
------------------------
Dan: That reminds me; I don’t know what I was thinking because I usually do it at the top of the call. I didn’t give you
guys a chance to give out websites and stuff. So if you want people to see what you’re doing, feel free to tell them now
where to go see it.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 14 --

11 | P a g e
Steve: You can go to AskPSP.com for our business and my book website is www.PassionateEntrepreneur.com.
Ben: You can see Dan Kennedy style marketing for a law practice at www.BenGlassLaw.com and you can see
the info-marketing business at www.GreatLegalMarketing.com.
------------------------
David: Hi, gentlemen, great call today. This is David Phelps, a former practicing dentist who was very down in the mouth
until I found Dan Kennedy about 10 years ago through his “No BS Time Management for Entrepreneurs”.
The second edition is now out and I highly recommend it for anyone who has not picked one up yet. I’m going to pick up
on what Steve said at the end there about leverage.
This is for dealing with professional practice owners, who much due to their educational process – which is obviously
very specialized training about the “what” that they do – seem to have a really difficult time replacing the mindset of
trading time for dollars to the Dan Kennedy mindset of time, value and leverage; that is getting things done through other
people – becoming the who and not the what. How do you help your clients and particularly Ben, how do you help your
attorney clients overcome that? Are there tricks; are there baby steps that you help them take to go beyond where they are
today?
Ben: I don’t think there are tricks. I think it’s an immersion process so I am always trying to get my story out there – nine
kids, two businesses and still get home in time for dinner. “If this goofball can do it, so can you; here’s how I think.” One
of the most popular things we do at Great Legal Marketing is every month we have our own mindset call where we’re not
talking about marketing we’re talking about this personal development stuff.
Most professionals don’t believe it when they hear it because they’ve never heard it before; so we teach them you’ve got
to hang out with people that are bigger, better and faster than you are. So go hang out at a GKIC group like Peak
Performers, form your own mastermind group and read those books. So it’s not tricks; for me at least it’s being exposed to
how successful people actually think over and over. So when they see a David Phelps, they see a Dr. Martin, they see Ben
Glass and they ask them “Do you have any problem with the fact that you only work 2 to 5 hours a week on clients’
cases?” I say, “No, not anymore!” I did at one point but not anymore.
Steve: I would just echo that and say that, first of all, you can’t make anyone want to do this. It has to be the kind of
person who has a greater appetite for results than they are currently getting and they feel the pain of it and those are the
best students. I know in our business, the managers that are struggling are always the ones that can’t let go and they’ve got
to control everything. They are not able to be successful until they can do that so I really think it is all about mindset. I’ve
got a friend of mine who is a GKIC Diamond member – Shane Smith in Atlanta – he’s a personal injury lawyer and he’s
made that transition; only five or six years in the business, I think, to multiple attorneys now and him not doing much
practice. But he was a guy that had that profile.
David: I agree; I think many people do not like the position they are in right now but they are not uncomfortable enough
to overcome the fear of the initial discomfort that change might create. So, you are right, association with others that have
been there and done it I think is the key.
Steve: The thing is it’s a lot of work to get there because what a lot of entrepreneurs want to do – I saw this a lot when I
was a banker – is they just want to abdicate instead of train and empower. They just dump the work and then go do their
thing and then they have to come back and clean it up. So, in the short run, it’s always more painful to get there.
Dan: Ben earlier said something important to any professional who is trying to make the transition, which is that he
essentially pokes his nose in at strategic points during the handling of the case, without actually handling it. I think
there’s a great deal of fear with dentists, cosmetic dentists, chiropractors, lawyers, CPAs, whatever, that the thing is
going to get screwed up. They have to have a process for making sure that it doesn’t.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 14 --

12 | P a g e
I also think – somewhat unique to the professional – that because, as you pointed out David, all their education has been
based on the clinical, on the technical, on the doing, that they have their ego entirely wrapped up in the clinical or the
technical. They are getting their psychic satisfaction from the crown well-done, the Implant well-done, the case well-done
rather than from achieving key metrics within the business in terms of sales, revenues, profits, growth and client or
patient scorecard satisfaction and all of that. Their attitude about that role has to be changed because they are ego-driven
folks and they have to have ego satisfaction in what they do or they are not going to do it.
David: Very true. Well fortunately I got over my crown margins and went on to better things but you’re exactly right so
that’s something that has to be taken care of. Thank you gentlemen.
------------------------
Julie: Thanks so much for all of this. It has been really inspiring. Today I was reading about accomplishment
entrepreneurs as being one of three categories and I would say you guys fit that. My question has to do I think Steve with
you. You mentioned a one-page business plan; can you highlight or share what some of the bullet points are?
Steve: Sure I’m not done with it yet but one of the things is I’m going to do a CEO Leadership Roundtable next year,
where I deliver a one-year leadership development track for our employees who are emerging leaders at the foundations
track and then I’ve got an advanced one. So that’s on there; that’s a big project for me next year that I’m working on.
Another one is I’ve got another book that I want to publish so I’ve got that on there and I’ve got some steps underneath
that I want to break down. Then Dan got after me from giving everything away free in my book content so I’m working
on a paid model for that.
Dan: Give it to me more as template. So all the managers have to give you a one-page business plan, is there a format for
that or are they freelancing or what?
Steve: There’s a format. I don’t know if you heard earlier, we have something called our playbook – its six things that we
want our store leaders to focus on. As long as they play in those areas, they’re going to be successful. When they get
outside of those is when they get fragmented and unsuccessful.
The format is they have to address the six areas in their business plan; it has to be one-page or less and it has to be
prioritized by quarter. I do the same thing except I can play everywhere because I’m not running a store. So mine is the
same thing; it is one-page, it’s prioritized by quarter.
It’s generally big things that will make a monetary difference to our company. I’m not just putting things there that are
moving paper piles. It’s something significant that has an impact on our profitability or my wealth. What I do from there
is then I have a separate legal pad for every one of those where I actually write out everything I can think of that I need to
do to get there; then that’s what I work from in building my weekly schedules.
Julie: So the six focuses, are they metrics or are they more soft goals?
Steve: No, they are pretty specific. They have to do 30-day action plans and they have to call their “churn customers” –
every customer that churns out from their store every month, they have to call; they have to build alliance partnerships, we
have this referral program with rescue groups and they have to build a 250-name personal list of clients they went out and
got themselves. They have to live and teach our mission every day. So those are some examples; I think there’s one more
in there in terms of operations excellence. They are very specific things but there are many things they can do to attack
those six things and therein lies the reason they do the business plan and only focus on those six things.
------------------------
Dan: Alright, thanks to everybody who was on the call; Steve, Ben thank you very, very much. Obviously you guys are
busy and we all appreciate the time and you are making the rest of us, including me, look bad! Thanks a lot guys.
www.getwsodownload.com
